---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
incremental: false
header-includes: |
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric, fit, backgrounds, calc}
  \usepackage{booktabs}
  \usepackage{xcolor}
  \definecolor{mblue}{RGB}{35,55,100}
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
\title{Clinical Research Methodology\\
\vspace{0.5em}
{\normalsize \textbf{SECTION 3 --- Causal Inference}}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}
\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\hfill \includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
}
\maketitle
```
:::

# 3.1 Correlation vs Causality

## The Fundamental Problem

\small

Observational clinical research produces statistical associations. But is an association sufficient to ground a therapeutic intervention?

\vspace{0.4em}

**Classic example**: ice cream and drownings are correlated. The correlation is real (both increase in summer), but ice cream does not cause drownings.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Neuropsychological example**: people with Alzheimer's have lower acetylcholine levels. Does this mean that increasing acetylcholine improves cognition? (Partial answer: yes, but with modest effects — the causality was more complex).

\vspace{0.4em}

**Bradford Hill Criterion** (1965): 9 criteria for evaluating evidence of causality (strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, dose-response, biological plausibility, coherence, experiment, analogy).

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Bradford Hill (1965). The environment and disease: association or causation? \emph{Proc R Soc Med} 58:295--300. Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1177/003591576505800503}

## The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference

\small

The **Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference** (Holland, 1986): we can never observe both **counterfactuals** for the same individual at the same moment.

\vspace{0.4em}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!20,text width=3.8cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=1cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (paz) at (0,2.5) {Patient with MCI};
\node[box] (t1)  at (-2.8,0.8) {Receives cognitive\\training};
\node[box] (t0)  at ( 2.8,0.8) {Does not receive\\cognitive training};
\node[box] (y1)  at (-2.8,-0.6) {Outcome $Y^{(1)}$\\(observed)};
\node[box] (y0)  at ( 2.8,-0.6) {Outcome $Y^{(0)}$\\(\textbf{counterfactual})};
\draw[arr] (paz)--(t1); \draw[arr] (paz)--(t0);
\draw[arr] (t1)--(y1);  \draw[arr] (t0)--(y0);
\node[font=\tiny,red!70] at (2.8,-1.4) {Not observable!};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

\small The individual causal effect = $Y^{(1)} - Y^{(0)}$. But we observe only one of the two. Randomization solves the problem \emph{on average} between groups.

# 3.2 DAG: Directed Acyclic Graphs

## What Is a DAG?

\small

A **DAG** (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a tool to explicitly represent **causal assumptions** on a system. Each arrow represents a hypothesis of direct causal relationship.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Components**:
\footnotesize
- **Nodes**: variables (observed or latent)
- **Arrows**: direct causal relationships
- **Acyclic**: there are no cycles (A $\to$ B $\to$ A)

\vspace{0.3em}
**Purpose**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Identify which variables to control for (and which \emph{not} to control)
\item Identify confounders, mediators, colliders
\item Assess whether a causal effect is identifiable from available data
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Textor et al.\ (2016). Robust causal reasoning using DAGs. \emph{Int J Epidemiol}.\\
Online tool: DAGitty --- Open Access: \url{https://www.dagitty.net}


## Fundamental Structures in DAGs

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=0.65cm,inner sep=1pt},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
% Chain (mediator)
\node[font=\small\bfseries] at (-4,3.5) {Chain (Mediator)};
\node[node] (A1) at (-5,2.5) {A};
\node[node] (M)  at (-4,2.5) {M};
\node[node] (B1) at (-3,2.5) {B};
\draw[arr] (A1)--(M); \draw[arr] (M)--(B1);
\node[font=\tiny] at (-4,1.9) {A $\to$ M $\to$ B};

% Fork (confounder)
\node[font=\small\bfseries] at (0,3.5) {Fork (Confounder)};
\node[node] (C)  at (0,2.8) {C};
\node[node] (A2) at (-1,2.0) {A};
\node[node] (B2) at (1,2.0)  {B};
\draw[arr] (C)--(A2); \draw[arr] (C)--(B2);
\node[font=\tiny,align=center] at (0,1.4) {C causes both A and B\\(spurious A--B association)};

% Collider
\node[font=\small\bfseries] at (4,3.5) {Collider};
\node[node] (A3) at (3,2.8) {A};
\node[node] (B3) at (5,2.8) {B};
\node[node] (K)  at (4,2.0) {K};
\draw[arr] (A3)--(K); \draw[arr] (B3)--(K);
\node[font=\tiny,align=center] at (4,1.4) {A and B both cause K\\(independent, but cond.\ on K:\\spurious association!)};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Confounder: Neuropsychological Example

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  obs/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!50,text=white,font=\footnotesize,
              minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  spurious/.style={-{Stealth},red!60,dashed,thick}
]
\node[obs] (eta)  at (0,3)   {Age};
\node[obs] (dep)  at (-2.5,1) {Depression};
\node[obs] (cog)  at ( 2.5,1) {Cognitive\\Performance};
\draw[arr]     (eta)--(dep);
\draw[arr]     (eta)--(cog);
\draw[spurious](dep)--(cog)
  node[midway,below,font=\tiny,red!70]{Spurious association?};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small Age causes both depression and cognitive decline. If we do not control for age, we find a depression--cognition association that could be \textbf{totally} or \textbf{partially} spurious.

\vspace{0.3em}
\textbf{Solution}: add age as a covariate in the regression --- or better, construct the DAG \emph{before} analyzing the data.

## Collider: A Subtle Error

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  spurious/.style={-{Stealth},red!60,dashed,very thick}
]
\node[node] (gen) at (-2.5,2) {Genetic\\factors\\(APOE)};
\node[node] (tbi) at ( 2.5,2) {Head trauma\\(TBI)};
\node[node] (ad)  at (0,0.5)  {Diagnosis\\of AD};
\draw[arr] (gen)--(ad);
\draw[arr] (tbi)--(ad);
\draw[spurious] (gen) to[bend left=25] node[above,font=\tiny,red!70, align=center]{Negative association\\(collider bias!)} (tbi);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small In a clinical sample (selected for having AD), APOE and TBI become **negatively correlated** even though in the general population they are independent. \textbf{Conditioning on a collider opens a spurious pathway}.

\vspace{0.2em}
\footnotesize \textbf{Practical implication}: studies conducted only on hospitalized patients or with a specific diagnosis may suffer from collider bias (also known as \emph{Berkson's bias}).

## Mediator: Not Always to Be Controlled

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.2cm,inner sep=2pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[node] (dep)  at (-2.5,1.5) {Depression};
\node[node] (som)  at (0,1.5)    {Daytime\\Sleepiness};
\node[node] (att)  at (2.5,1.5)  {Attention\\(worsened)};
\draw[arr] (dep)--(som);
\draw[arr] (som)--(att);
\draw[arr] (dep) to[bend right=30] (att);
\node[font=\tiny,mblue] at (0,0.3) {Direct + indirect (mediated) pathway};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small If we want to estimate the **total** effect of depression on attention, we should \textbf{not} control for sleepiness (which is a mediator). Doing so would block part of the causal effect.

\vspace{0.3em}
\footnotesize \textbf{Direct vs indirect effect}: mediation analysis (e.g., via SEM or packages like \texttt{mediation} in R) allows decomposition of the total effect.



# 3.3 Methods for Causal Inference from Observational Data

## Propensity Score

\small

The **propensity score** (Rosenbaum & Rubin, 1983) is the probability that a subject receives treatment, conditional on their observed characteristics.

$$PS_i = P(\text{Treatment}_i = 1 \mid X_i)$$

where $X_i$ is the vector of observed covariates.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Intuition**: subjects with the same propensity score are "quasi-randomized" with respect to observed covariates.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Methods of use**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{Matching}: match treated and control subjects with similar PS
\item \textbf{Stratification}: divide into PS quintiles
\item \textbf{Weighting} (IPW): weights inversely proportional to PS
\item \textbf{Covariate}: include PS as covariate in regression
\end{itemize}



## Propensity Score: Neuropsychological Example

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!15,text width=3.5cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=0.9cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (cov) at (0,3.5)  {Covariates:\\age, education,\\comorbidities, MMSE};
\node[box] (ps)  at (0,2.2)  {Estimate PS:\\logistic / random forest};
\node[box] (mat) at (0,1.0)  {Matching or\\IPW weighting};
\node[box] (out) at (0,-0.2) {Estimate causal\\treatment effect};
\draw[arr] (cov)--(ps); \draw[arr] (ps)--(mat); \draw[arr] (mat)--(out);
\node[right,font=\tiny,text width=3.5cm, align=center] at (2.0,3.5) {E.g.\ patients who\\ chose training\\vs no training};
\node[right,font=\tiny,text width=3.5cm, align=center] at (2.0,1.0) {Balances pre-\\treatment\\characteristics};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

\small \textbf{Key assumption (not verifiable!)}: \emph{no unmeasured confounders} --- all variables influencing both treatment selection and outcome have been measured and included in the PS.

## Propensity Score Limitations

\small

**Positivity assumption**: every subject must have probability $> 0$ of receiving each treatment. If some subjects have PS $= 0$ or $= 1$, comparison is not possible.

\vspace{0.3em}

**No unmeasured confounders**: the PS balances observed covariates, but not latent or unmeasured ones (e.g., patient motivation, unreported family support).

\vspace{0.3em}

**Insufficient overlap**: if treated and control subjects are very different, matching does not find adequate pairs and the estimate is unreliable.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Not an RCT**: the PS reduces bias from observed confounding, but does not eliminate it completely. It should always be accompanied by sensitivity analysis.

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Austin (2011). An introduction to propensity score methods. \emph{Multivariate Behav Res} 46(3). Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1080/00273171.2011.568786}


## Other Approaches to Causal Inference

\small

**Instrumental Variables (IV)**

An instrumental variable $Z$ influences treatment but not the outcome except through treatment. It allows estimating causal effects even in the presence of unmeasured confounders.

\emph{Example}: geographic variations in access to neurorehabilitation services as an instrument.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Difference-in-Differences (DiD)**

Compares the change over time between an exposed group and an unexposed group, controlling for pre-existing trends.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Sensitivity Analysis**

E-value analysis (VanderWeele & Ding, 2017): quantifies how strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be to explain the observed association.

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny VanderWeele & Ding (2017). Sensitivity analysis in observational research: introducing the E-value. \emph{Ann Intern Med}. Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.7326/M16-2607}

# 3.4 Theoretical Frameworks

## Potential Outcomes Framework (Rubin)

\small

The **potential outcomes** framework (Rubin, 1974; Holland, 1986) formalizes causality via counterfactual variables.

\vspace{0.3em}

For each subject $i$ and treatment $t \in \{0,1\}$:
$$Y_i^{(t)} = \text{outcome that would be observed if the subject received treatment } t$$

**Individual causal effect**: $\tau_i = Y_i^{(1)} - Y_i^{(0)}$

**Average Treatment Effect (ATE)**: $\tau = E[Y^{(1)} - Y^{(0)}]$

**ATE in the Treated (ATT)**: $\tau_{ATT} = E[Y^{(1)} - Y^{(0)} \mid T=1]$

\vspace{0.3em}
\small Randomization ensures that $E[Y^{(0)} \mid T=1] = E[Y^{(0)} \mid T=0]$ (exchangeability), making the counterfactual identifiable.

## Do-Calculus and Pearl's Framework

\small

Judea Pearl developed an alternative framework based on DAGs and **do-calculus** to answer causal questions.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Fundamental difference**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item $P(Y \mid X=x)$: observed probability of $Y$ given that we observe $X=x$ (statistic)
\item $P(Y \mid do(X=x))$: probability of $Y$ if we \emph{intervene} by setting $X=x$ (causal)
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Backdoor criterion**: a set of variables $Z$ blocks all "backdoor" paths between treatment and outcome $\Rightarrow$ controlling for $Z$ yields a causal estimate.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Practical tool**: **DAGitty** (\url{www.dagitty.net}) allows drawing DAGs online and automatically identifies the minimal adjustment set.

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Pearl (2009). \emph{Causality}. Cambridge University Press.\\
Free: Pearl et al.\ (2016). \emph{Causal Inference in Statistics: A Primer}. Wiley. Chapters available Open Access: \url{http://bayes.cs.ucla.edu/PRIMER/}


## Backdoor Criterion: Example

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.1cm,inner sep=1pt,align=center},
  blocked/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!50,text=white,font=\footnotesize,
                  minimum size=1.1cm,inner sep=1pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  back/.style={-{Stealth},red!50,dashed,thick}
]
\node[node]    (eta) at (0,3.5)   {Age};
\node[node]    (dep) at (-2.5,2)  {Depression\\(Treatment)};
\node[blocked] (cog) at (2.5,2)   {Cognition\\(Outcome)};
\node[node]    (att) at (0,0.5)   {Hippocampal\\Atrophy};

\draw[arr] (dep)--(cog) node[midway,above,font=\tiny]{?};
\draw[arr] (dep)--(att);
\draw[arr] (att)--(cog);
\draw[back] (eta)--(dep);
\draw[back] (eta)--(cog);
\draw[back] (dep) to[bend left=15] node[above,font=\tiny,red!60]{Backdoor path via age} (eta);

\node[font=\scriptsize,red!70,text width=4cm] at (0,-0.5)
  {Closing the age closes the backdoor path and permits causal estimate of depression $\to$ cognition};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Confusion Index: Summary of Structures

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{>{\footnotesize}l >{\footnotesize}l >{\footnotesize}p{4cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Structure} & \textbf{Control?} & \textbf{Effect if controlled} \\
\midrule
Confounder (fork)  & \textbf{Yes}  & Removes spurious association \\
Mediator (chain)  & \textbf{No}  & Blocks part of causal effect \\
Collider           & \textbf{No}  & Opens a spurious pathway \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\footnotesize \emph{NB: the choice depends on the causal question, not on statistical significance}} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.5em}
\small This table is the practical core of the course: in psychology and neuropsychology, adding control variables "because they are correlated" without a DAG can produce worse estimates than having no control at all.
```

---

# Section 3 Summary

## Summary: From Correlation to Causality

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!20,text width=3.4cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=0.85cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (q)  at (0,4.2) {Causal question\\(not just associative)};
\node[box] (dag) at (0,3.0) {DAG: represent\\causal assumptions};
\node[box] (id) at (0,1.8)  {Identification:\\backdoor criterion\\adjustment set};
\node[box] (est) at (0,0.6) {Estimation:\\PS matching, IPW,\\IV, DiD, RD};
\node[box] (sen) at (0,-0.6){Sensitivity analysis:\\E-value, RD bounds};
\draw[arr] (q)--(dag); \draw[arr] (dag)--(id);
\draw[arr] (id)--(est); \draw[arr] (est)--(sen);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Practical Open Access Tools — Section 3

\small

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize

\item \textbf{DAGitty} (Online DAG, free):\\
  \url{https://www.dagitty.net}

\item \textbf{Causal Inference in Statistics: A Primer} (Pearl et al.):
  Selected chapters Open Access:\\
  \url{http://bayes.cs.ucla.edu/PRIMER/}

\item \textbf{The Effect: An Introduction to Research Design and Causality}\\
  (Huntington-Klein, 2022). Entire book Open Access:\\
  \url{https://theeffectbook.net}

\item \textbf{Causal Inference: The Mixtape} (Cunningham, 2021).\\
  Open Access: \url{https://mixtape.scunning.com}

\item \textbf{Propensity Score}: Austin (2011). \emph{Multivariate Behav Res}.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1080/00273171.2011.568786}

\item \textbf{E-value}: VanderWeele & Ding (2017). \emph{Ann Intern Med}.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.7326/M16-2607}

\item \textbf{Bradford Hill criteria}: Hill (1965). \emph{Proc R Soc Med}.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1177/003591576505800503}

\item \textbf{R package \texttt{MatchIt}} (PS matching):
  Ho et al., Open Access:\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v042.i08}

\end{itemize}

-->
